description: Golden dataset for RAGReady evaluation. 50 Q&A pairs covering factual, comparative, and refusal categories.

entries.question: Who created the Python programming language?
entries.expected_answer: Python was created by Guido van Rossum.
expected_contexts: Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991

entries.question: When was Python first released?
entries.expected_answer: Python was first released in 1991.
expected_contexts: Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991

entries.question: What is the reference implementation of Python called?
entries.expected_answer: The reference implementation of Python is called CPython, which is written in C.
expected_contexts: The reference implementation of Python is called CPython, which is written in C

entries.question: What PEP introduced type hints in Python?
entries.expected_answer: Type hints were introduced in Python through PEP 484.
expected_contexts: type hints were introduced through PEP 484

entries.question: What is the time complexity for dictionary lookup in Python?
entries.expected_answer: Python dictionaries have O(1) average lookup time, implemented using hash tables.
expected_contexts: dictionary (dict) is an unordered collection of key-value pairs with O(1) average lookup time

entries.question: What module is used to create virtual environments in Python?
entries.expected_answer: The venv module, included in the Python standard library since Python 3.3, creates virtual environments.
expected_contexts: The venv module, included in the Python standard library since Python 3.3

entries.question: How many packages does PyPI host?
entries.expected_answer: The Python Package Index (PyPI) hosts over 500,000 third-party packages.
expected_contexts: Python Package Index (PyPI), which hosts over 500,000 third-party packages

entries.question: What principle does Python follow for exception handling?
entries.expected_answer: Python follows the EAFP principle (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission), preferring try-except over pre-checking conditions.
expected_contexts: Python follows the EAFP principle (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission)

entries.question: When was Python 3.0 released?
entries.expected_answer: Python 3.0 was released in December 2008.
expected_contexts: Python 3.0, released in December 2008, was a major backward-incompatible revision

entries.question: What are Python decorators?
entries.expected_answer: Decorators are a syntactic sugar for wrapping functions, using the @decorator_name syntax above the function definition.
expected_contexts: Decorators are a syntactic sugar for wrapping functions, using the @decorator_name syntax

entries.question: What does Python's match statement provide?
entries.expected_answer: Python 3.10 introduced the match statement for structural pattern matching, similar to switch-case statements.
expected_contexts: Python 3.10 introduced the match statement for structural pattern matching

entries.question: What are the characteristics of a Python set?
entries.expected_answer: A set is an unordered collection of unique elements supporting mathematical set operations with O(1) average membership testing.
expected_contexts: A set is an unordered collection of unique elements that supports mathematical set operations

entries.question: What HTTP status code means Not Found?
entries.expected_answer: HTTP status code 404 means Not Found.
expected_contexts: 404 means Not Found, and 500 means Internal Server Error

entries.question: What HTTP methods does REST use?
entries.expected_answer: HTTP methods include GET (retrieves data), POST (submits data), PUT (replaces a resource), PATCH (partially modifies), and DELETE (removes a resource).
expected_contexts: GET retrieves data from a server, POST submits data for processing, PUT replaces an existing resource

entries.question: Who created the FastAPI framework?
entries.expected_answer: FastAPI was created by Sebastián Ramírez.
expected_contexts: FastAPI is a modern Python web framework for building APIs, created by Sebastián Ramírez

entries.question: Where can you access FastAPI's auto-generated documentation?
entries.expected_answer: FastAPI generates Swagger UI documentation at /docs and ReDoc documentation at /redoc.
expected_contexts: Swagger UI (available at /docs) and ReDoc (available at /redoc)

entries.question: What are the six constraints of REST?
entries.expected_answer: The six constraints of REST are client-server architecture, statelessness, cacheability, uniform interface, layered system, and code on demand (optional).
expected_contexts: six constraints of REST are: client-server architecture, statelessness, cacheability, uniform interface

entries.question: What are the three parts of a JWT?
entries.expected_answer: JSON Web Tokens consist of three parts: a header, a payload, and a signature, separated by dots.
expected_contexts: JSON Web Tokens (JWT) consist of three parts: a header, a payload, and a signature

entries.question: What improvements did HTTP/2 introduce?
entries.expected_answer: HTTP/2, published in 2015, introduced multiplexing, header compression, and server push.
expected_contexts: HTTP/2, published in 2015, introduced multiplexing, header compression, and server push

entries.question: What is semantic versioning?
entries.expected_answer: Semantic versioning follows the MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format where MAJOR indicates breaking changes, MINOR adds features, and PATCH includes bug fixes.
expected_contexts: Semantic versioning (SemVer) follows the MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format

entries.question: Who maintains React?
entries.expected_answer: React is maintained by Meta.
expected_contexts: React (maintained by Meta), Angular (maintained by Google), and Vue.js (created by Evan You)

entries.question: What are common API versioning strategies?
entries.expected_answer: Common API versioning strategies include URL path versioning, query parameter versioning, custom header versioning, and content negotiation using Accept headers.
expected_contexts: URL path versioning (e.g., /api/v1/users), query parameter versioning, custom header versioning

entries.question: What is the time complexity for array access by index?
entries.expected_answer: Arrays provide O(1) random access time using index-based lookup.
expected_contexts: An array stores elements in contiguous memory locations, providing O(1) random access time

entries.question: What is the time complexity for linked list element access?
entries.expected_answer: Accessing a linked list element by index requires O(n) time since traversal from the head is necessary.
expected_contexts: accessing an element by index requires O(n) time since traversal from the head is necessary

entries.question: What is a hash table's average time complexity for lookup?
entries.expected_answer: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case time complexity for lookup operations.
expected_contexts: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case time complexity for insertion, lookup, and deletion

entries.question: What are the common hash collision resolution strategies?
entries.expected_answer: Common collision resolution strategies include chaining (using linked lists at each bucket) and open addressing (probing for the next available slot).
expected_contexts: collision resolution strategies include chaining (using linked lists at each bucket) and open addressing

entries.question: What is the BST search time complexity in the worst case?
entries.expected_answer: BST search degrades to O(n) in the worst case when the tree becomes unbalanced.
expected_contexts: BST search, insertion, and deletion take O(log n) time in the average case but degrade to O(n)

entries.question: What is an AVL tree?
entries.expected_answer: An AVL tree is a self-balancing BST that maintains a balance factor of at most 1, guaranteeing O(log n) operations.
expected_contexts: AVL tree is a self-balancing BST that maintains a balance factor of at most 1

entries.question: What is the time complexity of BFS and DFS?
entries.expected_answer: Both BFS and DFS run in O(V + E) time where V is vertices and E is edges.
expected_contexts: Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores vertices level by level using a queue, running in O(V + E) time

entries.question: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm?
entries.expected_answer: Dijkstra's algorithm runs in O((V + E) log V) time using a priority queue.
expected_contexts: Dijkstra's algorithm finds the shortest path from a source vertex to all other vertices in O((V + E) log V)

entries.question: What is a B-tree used for?
entries.expected_answer: A B-tree is a self-balancing tree optimized for disk-based storage, commonly used in database indexes and file systems.
expected_contexts: B-tree is a self-balancing tree optimized for disk-based storage, commonly used in database indexes

entries.question: What Python module provides heap operations?
entries.expected_answer: Python provides the heapq module for min-heap operations on regular lists.
expected_contexts: Python provides the heapq module for min-heap operations on regular lists

entries.question: What is the lower bound for comparison-based sorting?
entries.expected_answer: Comparison-based sorting algorithms have a lower bound of O(n log n) time complexity.
expected_contexts: Comparison-based sorting algorithms have a lower bound of O(n log n) time complexity

entries.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of quick sort?
entries.expected_answer: Quick sort has O(n²) worst-case time complexity.
expected_contexts: Quick sort selects a pivot element and partitions the array around it, achieving O(n log n) average time but O(n²) worst-case

entries.question: What is the space difference between adjacency list and adjacency matrix?
entries.expected_answer: An adjacency list uses O(V + E) space while an adjacency matrix uses O(V²) space.
expected_contexts: adjacency list represents a graph by storing each vertex's neighbors in a list, using O(V + E) space

entries.question: When does a hash table resize?
entries.expected_answer: A hash table resizes when the load factor exceeds a threshold (typically 0.75), doubling its capacity and rehashing all entries.
expected_contexts: When the load factor exceeds a threshold (typically 0.75), the hash table resizes by doubling its capacity

entries.question: How do Python dictionaries compare to hash tables in data structures?
entries.expected_answer: Python dictionaries are implemented using hash tables, providing O(1) average lookup time, and since Python 3.7 they maintain insertion order.
expected_contexts: Python dictionaries and sets are implemented using hash tables
dictionary (dict) is an unordered collection of key-value pairs with O(1) average lookup time

entries.question: How does FastAPI use Python type hints differently from standard Python type hints?
entries.expected_answer: While Python type hints (PEP 484) are optional and don't affect runtime, FastAPI uses them for automatic request validation and serialization through Pydantic models.
expected_contexts: type hints were introduced through PEP 484
FastAPI uses Python type hints for automatic request validation and serialization through Pydantic models

entries.question: Compare array access time with hash table lookup time.
entries.expected_answer: Both arrays and hash tables provide O(1) time complexity for access/lookup — arrays use index-based access while hash tables use key-based hashing.
expected_contexts: array stores elements in contiguous memory locations, providing O(1) random access time
Hash tables provide O(1) average-case time complexity for insertion, lookup, and deletion

entries.question: How do REST APIs and Python functions both handle input validation?
entries.expected_answer: REST APIs use Pydantic models in FastAPI for automatic validation, while Python uses try-except blocks following the EAFP principle for handling invalid inputs.
expected_contexts: FastAPI uses Python type hints for automatic request validation
Python follows the EAFP principle (Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission)

entries.question: What are the tradeoffs between using arrays versus linked lists for insertion?
entries.expected_answer: Array insertion at an arbitrary position requires O(n) time due to shifting elements, while linked list insertion at the head takes O(1) time but requires O(n) to find a specific position.
expected_contexts: insertion at an arbitrary position requires O(n) time due to shifting elements
Linked list insertion and deletion at the head take O(1) time

entries.question: What is the best machine learning framework for image classification?
How do I set up a PostgreSQL database?
What is the best cloud provider for deploying microservices?
How do neural networks learn through backpropagation?
What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
How does Kubernetes manage container orchestration?
What is the CAP theorem in distributed systems?
How do I configure a Redis cache for session management?
What are the differences between TCP and UDP protocols?
Explain the MapReduce programming model for big data processing.
